pattern: The maximum subsequence sum takes every positive element, and every other sum is that maximum minus a chosen subset of absolute values, so a best-first search over those reductions finds the k-th largest.
hints: The largest possible sum keeps all positive numbers; flipping any choice only ever subtracts from it.
Replace each number by its absolute value and sort; every reachable sum is the maximum minus some subset sum of these magnitudes.
Start from the maximum and use a min-heap of (reduction, index) pairs to enumerate the smallest subset reductions in order, popping k-1 times to reach the k-th largest sum.

edge_inputs.description: k equals one returns the maximum sum
all negative, maximum is the empty subsequence

stress.description: moderate array with mixed signs and a deep but valid k
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 18))
    nums = [rng.randint(-50, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    total = 2 ** n
    k = rng.randint(1, min(2000, total))
    return [nums, k]

solutions.python:
import heapq
class Solution:
    def kSum(self, nums, k):
        maxsum = sum(v for v in nums if v > 0)
        mags = sorted(abs(v) for v in nums)
        n = len(mags)
        heap = [(mags[0], 0)] if n else []
        result = maxsum
        for _ in range(k - 1):
            if not heap:
                break
            red, i = heapq.heappop(heap)
            result = maxsum - red
            if i + 1 < n:
                heapq.heappush(heap, (red + mags[i + 1], i + 1))
                heapq.heappush(heap, (red - mags[i] + mags[i + 1], i + 1))
        return result

complexity.time: O(n log n + k log k)
